summaries.Name: Sark is one of the Channel Islands, a dependency of Guernsey, located in the English Channel south of England's coast, lying 7 miles (11 km) east of Guernsey and about 25 miles (40 km) west of the Cherbourg Peninsula of France. The island is 3 miles (5 km) long and 1.5 miles (2.4 km) wide at its broadest point, consisting of two components, Great Sark and Little Sark, connected by La Coupée, a 300-foot-long isthmus only about 30 feet wide. The smaller, privately owned island of Brecqhou is separated from Great Sark by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel, and together with Brecqhou, Sark covers a total area of 2.1 square miles (5.5 square km), with a population of 591 as of 2001. Sark is part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey and is essentially a plateau rising to 375 feet (114 metres), with a scenic coast encircled by precipitous cliffs and three small harbours: Creux, La Maseline, and Havre Gosselin. Human settlement is scattered, dairying is practiced, and about a third of the island is farmland with substantial tillage and some market gardening. Tourism is important to Sark's economy, though accommodation is limited and most visitors come on day trips from Guernsey or on cruises. Automobiles are prohibited on Sark's roads, with farm tractors being the only motor vehicles allowed, while horse-drawn carriages and bicycles are common means of transportation, and the island may be reached only by boat since airplanes are banned. Brecqhou is legally part of Sark's jurisdiction, though its jurisdictional status has been historically contested, and Dame Sibyl Hathaway is noted to have illegally bestowed upon Brecqhou the status of a Sark Tenement together with a seat in Chief Pleas.
summaries.Geography: Sark is one of the Channel Islands, a dependency of Guernsey, located in the English Channel south of England's coast, lying 7 miles (11 km) east of Guernsey and about 25 miles (40 km) west of the Cherbourg Peninsula of France. The island is 3 miles (5 km) long and 1.5 miles (2.4 km) wide at its broadest point, consisting of two components, Great Sark and Little Sark, connected by La Coupée, a 300-foot- (90-metre-) long isthmus only about 30 feet (10 metres) wide. The smaller, privately owned island of Brecqhou is separated from Great Sark by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel. Sark is part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey and is essentially a plateau rising to 375 feet (114 metres), with a scenic coast encircled by precipitous cliffs. The total area including Brecqhou is 2.1 square miles (5.5 square km), with a population of 591 as of 2001. Both the geography of the Sark/Brecqhou dyad and the fact that Brecqhou is a private island with just a tiny population in comparison with the island of Sark offers a context where separation by water helps to complicate perceptions of jurisdiction. Human settlement on the island is scattered, farmland covers about a third of the island, automobiles are prohibited on roads, and the island may be reached only by boat since airplanes are banned.
summaries.Feudal relationship with Sark: Brecqhou's feudal relationship with Sark is historically contested, with Dame Sibyl Hathaway having allegedly illegally bestowed upon Brecqhou the status of a Sark Tenement together with a seat in Chief Pleas, while legal scholar Dawes suggests that Brecqhou is not a "tenement of Sark." Legal advice provided by Guernsey advocates Carey & Son stated that "the laws of the Island of Sark, of which Brecqhou is a dependency, are an important point to be considered by prospective purchasers," and that "the tenure of Brecqhou is similar to that of the other tenements in Sark, that is to say that the owner is the tenant in perpetuity." An advocate overseeing the 1949 sale of Brecqhou noted that "strictly speaking, the owner of the above Island is called tenant, but in effect, he enjoys a freehold holding, although legally speaking, he has but a manorial freehold." The Barclay brothers, owners of Brecqhou, engaged in a long-running campaign to end Sark's feudal regime, ultimately resulting in the Privy Council agreeing to introduce universal voting rights and a 28-member assembly on Sark, with the brothers financing the island's monthly newspaper Sark News, which called for an end to the "deeply undemocratic, self-interested and self-serving regime" on the island.
summaries.Fort Brecqhou: Fort Brecqhou is the largest house built in Britain in the last 200 years, situated on Brecqhou Island, Guernsey, and is a granite-clad castle on a quadrangular plan built in the castellated Gothic style, complete with circular turrets at the four corners, featuring giant twisted chimneys of the style developed during the reign of Henry VIII, a chimney style unique to English architecture. The tenement of Brecqhou was purchased by the Barclay brothers, Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay, in 1993 for £2.3 million, with the estimated cost of building the house, gardens, and outbuildings reaching £90 million. The house was designed by architect John Quinlan Terry (born 1937, British nationality), with construction starting in 1994 and completing in 1996. The property is fully extant, currently serves as a private home and seat of Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay, is held under individual or family trust ownership, and is not listed. The grounds are open to the public by appointment only, and the house is not a Historic Houses member. The architecture of Fort Brecqhou is referenced in the publication "Radical Classicism: The Architecture of Quinlan Terry" by David Watkin, published in 2006 by Rizzoli International Publications.
summaries.Flag and stamps: The Bailiwick of Guernsey is a British jurisdiction in the Channel Islands comprising several islands, and within this archipelago a sense of territory and island identity is constructed with three main political jurisdictions (Guernsey, Alderney and Sark), and several leased or administered islands including Herm, Jethou, Brecqhou and Lihou. Each island has constructed a sense of place by utilising symbols associated with nation building, such as anthems, flags, crests, currency, and postage stamps, with jurisdictional politics operating within, between and across the space of these islands. The study of anthems and stamps in the Bailiwick of Guernsey is framed within a broader context of place branding, where politics is a recognised theme concerning autonomous and anomalous island territories, and where local identity branding draws on such civic and national symbols. The Sark/Brecqhou dyad is of particular interest in this context, as the relationship between the owners of Brecqhou and the jurisdiction of Sark has produced jurisdictional contestation, tension and criticism, making it a notable case study within Island Studies for examining how symbols including flags and stamps function as instruments of jurisdictional and political identity construction.
summaries.Public visits: No relevant information regarding Public visits is available.
